rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF LUCILLE A. WILLIAMS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: ACCOUNTING #2, REPORT, FEES
rulings.Rulings: Appearance required. NB: the following are outstanding procedural issues: 1) An incomplete "Amendment to Second Account" was filed on 7/17/18. Petitioner attached a complete copy to her Declaration filed on 9/14/18, which is insufficient. Filing will be ignored as it was unverified, unless a complete document is filed and titled "Amended Amendment to Second Account". 2) Petitioner alleges in her 9/14/18 Declaration that copies of bank statements have been filed, which is insufficient in conservatorship proceedings. Original bank statements must be filed. 3) Proposed order has not been submitted.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: ROBERT A. NAIFY LIVING TRUST DATED FEBRUARY 8, 1991
rulings.Calendar Matter: John Sherwoods (In His Individual Capacity Only) Notice Of General And Special Demurrers And General And Special Demurrers To Fourth Amended Petition For Relief
rulings.Rulings: John Sherwood's ("Sherwood") General and Special Demurrer to Acela Cortese's Fourth Amended Petition, brought in Sherwood's individual capacity, is SUSTAINED on all grounds. Acela's second cause of action, though titled a "third-party liability for breach of trust" claim, is in essence a civil conspiracy claim. As such, under Civ. Code §1714.10(a), Acela should have obtained a prefiling order before including that cause of action in her respective petitions. Acela has failed to allege sufficient facts showing that Sherwood owed her an "independent legal duty" or that he profited over and above his ordinary fees for services performed as Robert Naify's ("Robert") attorney when he allegedly participated in the breach of Robert's duties as trustee. (See Civ. Code § 1714.10(c).) In light of the analysis above and the Court of Appeal's directions to this Court in Cortese v. Sherwood, the Court is thus inclined to sustain Sherwood's demurrer with leave for Acela to make an application under § 1714.10(a) to include that cause of action. However, given the Court's tentative ruling on the statute of limitations issues, discussed below, Acela's second cause of action appears to be barred by the applicable statutes of limitations. Sherwood's demurrer to Acela's fraud claims (i.e., her fourth and fifth causes of action) is sustained without leave to amend. It is well established that fraud claims are subject to a stricter pleading standard. (See Glaski v. Bank of America, N.A. (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1079, 1090 [elements of fraud "may not be pleaded in a general or conclusory fashion."].) "'In an action for [common law] fraud, damage is an essential element of the cause of action.' ¿ "At the pleading stage, the complaint 'must show a cause and effect relationship between the fraud and damages sought; otherwise no cause of action is stated.'" (Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 202 [internal citations omitted].) Acela has failed to allege sufficient facts to show a causal connection between the alleged misrepresentations made by John and Robert and the harm she suffered, let alone the "complete causal relationship" required under applicable case law. (Ibid. ["A 'complete causal relationship' between the fraud or deceit and the plaintiff's damages is required."].) The facts alleged regarding causal damages are insufficient, despite this being the third iteration of Acela's petition, and Acela has made no attempt to set forth "additional facts to be pleaded" showing "that there is a reasonable possibility the defect can be cured to state a cause of action." (CCP 430.41(e)(1).) This being the fourth amendment to Acela's petition -and all amendments having been made after the filing of demurrer(s) - the Court finds it inappropriate to grant additional leave to amend at this juncture. Sherwood's demurrer to the second, fourth, and fifth causes of action on the grounds that those claims are barred by the applicable statutes of limitations is also sustained without leave to amend. Francesca died in 1997. Her trust was ostensibly funded in or around that time. The Termination Agreement negotiations took place in 2008 and 2009. Acela filed her Original Petition (to which this petition relates back) on April 6, 2017, and thus waited over eight years to bring her claims against Sherwood. Though Acela includes multiple allegations regarding "delayed discovery," i.e., that "despite her due diligence" she only learned of Robert and Sherwood's alleged breaches after Robert's death in 2016 and after she "gained her freedom" from addiction and incarceration, she fails to include any further specificity regarding what due diligence she performed in an effort to learn whether or not she had claims against Sherwood, or even when she was freed from addiction or incarceration.
